P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Administration ••

(916) 322·5662 
322·5660 
322-5901 
322-6441 
December 9, 1983 
Karl P. Hinderer 
Vice President and Director 
of Planning 
Joel Silverman and Associates 
20300 Ventura Blvd., suite 350 
Woodland Hills, CA 
91364 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-255 
Dear Mr. Hinderer: 
Thank you for your request for advice on Government Code 
Sections 87400-87405. 1/ 
The facts, as stated in your letter, 
are as follows: 
1. 
In 1979, S&S Construction Company applied to the South 
Coast Regional Coastal Commission to be allowed to subdivide 
some land in Laguna Niguel. 
At the time, you were a Planner 
on the Aliso Canyon Local Coastal Program and you spoke with 
the applicant. 
This application was never formally filed. 
2. 
In 1980, S&S Construction reapplied to the Regional 
Commission for the permit to subdivide. 
This revised 
application was reviewed by a Coastal Program Analyst whom 
you supervised. 
Your position, at that time, was Permit 
Chief for the South Coast Regional Commission. 
You were in 
charge of all permit activity. 
You attended some of the 
meetings between the applicant and the Analyst. 
The 
Analyst's report to the Commission was critically reviewed 
by someone other than yourself. 2/ 
You performed a 
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
2/ 
You indicated that the report was critically reviewed 
by the Chief Planner. 
The reason for the review was that the 
application dealt with the adjacent Aliso Canyon, which was the 
subject of a then pending Local Coastal Program. 


Karl P. Hinderer 
December 9, 1983 
Page 2 
standard review for completeness and standard editing. 
The 
application was approved by the South Coast Regional 
Commission, but was allowed to lapse by the applicant. 
3. 
You are now privately employed by Joel Silverman and 
Associates. 
S&S Construction Company is preparing to submit 
a new application for the same type of project 
(single-family residential), on the same site as the 
original project. 
The application must now be approved by 
the State Coastal Commission, rather than a Regional 
Commission. 3/ 
The standard of review has changed from 
that contained in the Coastal Act of 1976, to that 
prescribed by the Local Coastal Program. 
This new standard 
is "substantially different"4/ from the previous standard. 
Your question is whether Sections 87400-87405 prevent you 
from representing S&S Construction Company on their new 
application. 
You also, generally, asked how Section 87404 
applies to a violation of these provisions. 
DISCUSSION 
Section 87402 provides: 
No former state administrative official, after the 
termination of his or her employment or term of office, 
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or 
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the 
State of California) before any court or state 
administrative agency or any officer or employee 
thereof: 
(a) 
By making any formal or informal appearance, 
or by making any oral or written communication with the 
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, 
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if~ 
3/ The Regional Commissions were abolished. 
4/ You stated in a telephone conversation that the major 
issues for reviewing a project, under the Coastal Act of 1976 
and the Local Coastal program, are "substantially different." 
The Coastal Act provides many, general standards for reviewing a 
project, whereas the Local Coastal Program authorizes a 
particular type of project on a specific site and the review is 
focused on whether a proposed project satisfies the requirements 
for that site. 


Karl P. Hinderer 
December 9, 1983 
page 3 
(b) 
The State of California is a party or has a 
direct and substantial interest; and 
(c) 
If the proceeding is one in which the former 
state administrative official participated. 
Section 87402 provides: 
No former state administrative official, after 
the termination of his or her employment or term of 
office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, 
consult or assist in representing any other person 
(except the State of California) in any proceeding in 
which the official would be prohibited from appearing 
under Section 87401. 
You are a "former state administrative official" and a 
coastal permit application is a "proceeding" covered by these 
sections. 
(Section 87400(b) and (c).) 
The remaining issues are 
(1) whether the present application and the earlier applications 
are part of the same proceeding, and (2) whether this proceeding 
was one in which you "participated." 
As to the first question, the Fair Political Practices 
Commission generally interprets the word "proceeding," in 
connection with the Coastal Commission, to mean a specific 
permit application, including all of the procedural stages 
involved in the application. 
A new permit application, even if 
it involves the same tract of land, or some of the same issues, 
as a prior application, is ordinarily considered a new 
proceeding. 
(See the enclosed copy of the Galanter advice 
letter, A-82-079.) 
In the present situation, the same tract of land is involved 
in the permit applications, but the issues which must be 
addressed in the new application are substantially different 
than those previously addressed, due to the change in the 
standard of approval. 
In addition, an entirely different body 
will decide the new request. 
Thus, we conclude that the present 
application is part of a new proceeding. 
You are not prohibited 
from representing S&S Construction Company on this new 
application. 
The outcome on the first question makes it unnecessary for 
us to answer the second question as to whether you 
"participated" in the original proceedinif. 
In your letter, you asked how Section 87404 applied to 
violations of Sections 87401 and 87402. 
An individual who 
violates these provisions is subject to the enforcement 


Karl P. Hinderer 
December 9, 1983 
page 4 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (Sections 91000, 
~ seq.). 
In addition, Section 87404 allows any interested 
person or party, the court or the presiding or other officer, in 
any covered proceeding, to exclude an individual found to be in 
violation of these provisions from further participation, or 
from assisting or counseling any other participant, in the 
pending proceeding. 
Before the exclusion can be effectuated, 
the individual must be provided with notice and an opportunity 
to be heard. 
I hope I have answered all of your questions for you. If! 
can be any additional help, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
!l~h~~~ 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


October 27, 1983 
Mr. Robert M. Stern 
General Counsel 
CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL 
PRACTICES COMMISSION 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Mr. Stern: 
In 1979, S & S Construction Company applied to the South Coast 
Regional Commission for a subdivision of land in Laguna Niguel~ 
and I was a Planner on the Aliso Local Coastal Program for the 
area. 
The original application was never formally filed, and the 
applicants reapplied in 1980. 
During that time, I was the Permit 
Chief for the South Coast Regional Commission in charge of all 
permit activities. 
At the time of the original application, I had initially spoke 
with the applicants: 
But there was no formal review. 
The revised 
application was reviewed in 1980 by a Coastal Program Analyst, 
who worked under me. 
I did attend some of the meetings with the 
applicants and the Analyst. 
The Analyst's report to the Commission 
was critically reviewed by David Smith, the Chief Planner, because 
the major issues in the application dealt with adjacent Aliso Canyon, 
which was the subject of a then pending Local Coastal Program (LCP). 
I did, however, make my normal review for completeness and standard 
editing. 
The permit was approved by the South Coast Regional Com-
mission but was allowed to lapse by the applicant at the end of its 
two-year period. 
is whether my involvement in this 
tituted a 
and substantial 
in 
cons-
ect. 
The 
now 
to submit a new application for a 
project on the same site that is similar to the 
project. 
to 
State Coastal Commission; 
submitted to the 


Mr. Robert M. Stern 
General Counsel 
CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL 
PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Continued 
Page two 
where it was reviewed by the Commission staff for its compliance 
with the Coastal Act of 1976 and Regional Interpretive Guidelines. 
Subsequent to the original approval, the Commission has certified 
the Local Coastal Programs that cover the site and the adjacent 
Aliso Canyon; and the standard of review by the Commission staff is 
now those LCP's. 
I had no significant personal involvement in the 
Commission approval process of either of the LCP's. 
In your letter of December 4, 1980, to Roy Gorman (paragraph 3), 
you indicate that former" ... employees can represent the permit 
applicant on a new permit hearing after the original permit has 
lapsed ... " 
Could I now represent S G S Construction Company on 
their new permit? 
Finally, it is my understanding the statute provides only that a 
person who violates the article can be excluded from the hearing 
by the presiding officer if the violation is substantiated after 
proper notice and a public hearing. 
Thank you for your help in this matter. 
Sincerely, 
Karl P. Hinderer 
Vice President &, 
Director of 
cc: 
Coastal Commission,

Division 


tate of Califorllia

Karl P. Hinderer

Joel Silverman & Associates 
20300 Ventura Blvd., Ste. 350 
Woodland Hills, CA 
91364 
Re: 
A 
3-255 
Dear Mr. Hinderer: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has 
referred to Janis Shank McLean, an 
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
dvice request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice 
Therefore, unless your request 
legal questions, or unless more 
answer your request, you should

In addition, an entirely different body will decide the new request. Thus, we conclude that the present application is part of a new proceeding. You are not prohibited from representing S&S Construction Company on this new application.

sections.question_synthetic: May a former state administrative official who previously worked on permit applications for S&S Construction Company now privately represent the company on a substantially similar new permit application before a different state agency with changed review standards?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The new permit application constitutes a separate proceeding from the prior applications due to substantially different review standards and a different deciding body, so the former official is not prohibited from representing the company. Violations would be subject to enforcement provisions and could result in exclusion from the proceeding with proper notice and opportunity to be heard.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Complete advice letter from FPPC dated December 9, 1983. Document includes both the incoming request letter (October 27, 1983) and the FPPC response. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Minor formatting issues with footnotes but content is extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether Sections 87400-87405 prevent you from representing S&S Construction Company on their new application. You also, generally, asked how Section 87404 applies to a violation of these provisions.

CONCLUSION: In the present situation, the same tract of land is involved in the permit applications, but the issues which must be addressed in the new application are substantially different than those previously addressed, due to the change in the standard of approval. In addition, an entirely different body will decide the new request. Thus, we conclude that the present application is part of a new proceeding. You are not prohibited from representing S&S Construction Company on this new application.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Karl Hinderer, a former state administrative official with the Coastal Commission, may represent S&S Construction Company on a new coastal permit application despite his prior involvement with the company's earlier applications, because the new application constitutes a separate proceeding with substantially different standards of review and a different deciding agency.
